title: Chapter 7 Master Quiz
description: Test your mastery of Data Structures: Arrays and Objects.

questions.question: What brackets are used to create an Array?
questions.explanation: Square Brackets [ ] define an Array.

questions.question: What brackets are used to create an Object?
questions.explanation: Curly Braces { } define an Object and its key-value pairs.

questions.question: What is the primary difference between storing data in an Array versus an Object?
options: Arrays can hold numbers, objects hold strings
Arrays organize data by numbered index, objects use labeled string Keys
Arrays are faster, objects are slower
Arrays can be empty, objects cannot
questions.explanation: Arrays are unlabelled, ordered lists (0, 1, 2). Objects are unordered, labelled dictionaries (name: 'Alice').

questions.question: What index does an Array start counting from?
questions.explanation: Arrays are universally Zero-Indexed in JavaScript. The first item is always [0].

questions.question: If let cars = ["Ford", "Honda", "Chevy"]; what is cars[1]?
questions.explanation: 0 is Ford. 1 is Honda. 2 is Chevy.

questions.question: The `.length` property of an array tells you the exact index number of the final item in the array.
questions.explanation: .length tells you the TOTAL COUNT of items (e.g., 3). Because arrays are zero-indexed, the last item's index is always (length - 1).

questions.question: Which array method attaches a new item specifically to the absolute END of the array?
questions.explanation: .push() adds to the end. .unshift() adds to the front.

questions.question: Which array method destroys and removes the absolute FIRST item from the array (Index 0)?
questions.explanation: .shift() removes from the front. .pop() removes from the back.

questions.question: You have an array with 10 items. You run `.pop()` once. What is the new `.length` of the array?
questions.explanation: .pop() removes one item from the end, shrinking the total length to 9.

questions.question: Inside an object dictionary, data is stored as perfect pairs consisting of a ___ and a Value.
questions.explanation: Objects use Key-Value pairs (e.g., name: 'Alice').

questions.question: What is the most common and readable way to access data trapped inside an Object's keys?
questions.explanation: Dot notation (e.g., user.age) is the cleanest, most standard way to access object properties.

questions.question: When would you be FORCED to use Bracket Notation (e.g. obj["key"]) instead of Dot Notation?
options: When the value is a number
When the key contains a space (like 'First Name')
When the object is inside an array
questions.explanation: Dot notation cannot process spaces! If a key has a space, you must enclose it in strings inside brackets.

questions.question: The `.forEach()` method is an older, bulkier loop structure that requires you to manually track an index like 'i'.
questions.explanation: .forEach() is the modern, CLEANER alternative precisely because it handles the index ('i') behind the scenes automatically for you.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the `.map()` method on an array?
options: To locate the index of a specific item
To iterate over the array and return a brand new, magically transformed array
To delete the array from memory
questions.explanation: While .forEach() simply runs code, .map() gathers all returned values to build a fresh, modified array.

questions.question: Look at: `let users = [ {id: 1, val: "A"}, {id: 2, val: "B"} ];`. What does `users[1].val` output?
questions.explanation: users[1] grabs the SECOND object in the array. Then '.val' grabs the value 'B'.

questions.question: What is wrong with this object? `let x = { name="John", age=50 }`
options: It must use semicolons, not commas
It uses equals (=) instead of colons (:) to link keys and values
questions.explanation: Objects link keys and values with a colon (:). E.g., { name: 'John', age: 50 }

questions.question: It is completely fine and normal to store an Array inside an Object.
questions.explanation: Yes! Data structures can nest infinitely. { user: 'Alice', hobbies: ['Reading', 'Running'] }

questions.question: What is the output? `let arr = []; console.log(arr[0]);`
questions.explanation: If you try to access an index that doesn't exist, JavaScript simply returns 'undefined'.

questions.question: What happens when you overwrite an array's item? `let x=[1, 2]; x[0]=99;`
questions.explanation: Arrays are perfectly mutable. Reassigning an index destroys the old piece of data.

questions.question: If `myList.length` is 5, what is the index of the absolute last item?
questions.explanation: Because it starts at 0, an array of 5 items sits at indexes 0, 1, 2, 3, and 4. (Length - 1)
